Contact Profile: <From Bulldog> von Zech coordinates afternoon news coverage. "We are primarily a business network, but we also like lifestyle, wealth stories and things that have consumer interest," he says. "Once the markets open, our focus shifts away from lifestyle stories. We'll do more business - oriented stories pertaining to companies that are trading and how they are affecting the trading day," he says. "The best PR pros understand the immediacy of radio. They have somebody available to speak immediately on what they are pitching. They avoid using `urgent' in the pitch. It might be urgent to the company that's putting it out, but to me it's just kind of like the boy crying wolf," he says. He recently used a survey from "The Pew Trust" on how middle-class income people felt they weren't getting ahead in the last five years.

Contact Profile: Mr. Walker has been with WBGO for over 20 years. <From Bulldog> "Our region consists of nine counties in N.J., five bureaus of Manhattan and also into the Northwest," Walker says. "We're basically covering the N.Y. and N.J. metropolitan areas and reaching beyond that, and the Northwest." What's useful about PR is brevity. Press releases often contain too much information. Just give who, what, why, when and where. Give him four to six weeks lead-time. "We really don't use products much," he says. He prefers contact through standard mail or email. If following up on a news item, talk to the News director. Contact him between 9 a.m. and 5 p.m. EST.
